verses.text: In the seventeenth year of Pekah the son of Remaliah Ahaz the son of Jotham king of Judah began to reign. Twenty years old was Ahaz when he began to reign, and reigned sixteen years in Jerusalem, and did not that which was right in the sight of the LORD his God, like David his father. But he walked in the way of the kings of Israel, yea, and made his son to pass through the fire, according to the abominations of the heathen, whom the LORD cast out from before the children of Israel. And he sacrificed and burnt incense in the high places, and on the hills, and under every green tree. Guidance: Ahaz — the worst king Judah has yet had. After the good reigns of Uzziah and Jotham, the Davidic line takes a catastrophic turn. Ahaz, son of the faithful Jotham, 'did not that which was right in the sight of the LORD his God, like David his father.' The verdict drops below the usual 'did right but the high places remained'; Ahaz is positively evil. He 'walked in the way of the kings of Israel' — adopting the apostasy of the northern kingdom into Judah. And he crosses a line no prior Judean king had crossed: 'he made his son to pass through the fire' — child sacrifice, the most horrific of the Canaanite 'abominations of the heathen' (the Molech-worship that consumed children, Lev 18:21; the very practice for which the LORD had cast out the original inhabitants). He also 'sacrificed and burnt incense in the high places, and on the hills, and under every green tree' — the full apparatus of Canaanite fertility-cult worship. The chapter is showing us a son of a godly father plunging into the worst idolatry — the reverse of Jotham, who took his father Uzziah's good and refused his sin. Ahaz takes the whole inheritance of the covenant and throws it away. The chapter is teaching us that godliness is not hereditary: a faithful father (Jotham) can produce a faithless son, and the covenant promises do not automatically transmit through the bloodline. The chapter is also sounding the alarm for Judah — the southern kingdom, which had been distinguished from the apostate north, now has a king walking 'in the way of the kings of Israel.' If Judah becomes like Israel, the same exile awaits. The chapter is teaching us that the most flagrant evil (child sacrifice) often arrives precisely when a leader abandons the God of his fathers for the gods of the surrounding culture.

Then Rezin king of Syria and Pekah son of Remaliah king of Israel came up to Jerusalem to war: and they besieged Ahaz, but could not overcome him. At that time Rezin king of Syria recovered Elath to Syria, and drave the Jews from Elath: and the Syrians came to Elath, and dwelt there unto this day. Guidance: The Syro-Ephraimite siege. The threat that 'began' under Jotham (15:37) now bursts into open war. Rezin of Aram and Pekah of Israel — the anti-Assyrian coalition — march on Jerusalem to force Judah to join their alliance against Assyria (and, per Isa 7:6, to depose Ahaz and install a puppet, the 'son of Tabeal'). They besiege the city 'but could not overcome him' — Jerusalem holds. Meanwhile Rezin recaptures the southern port of Elath (which Uzziah had built up, 14:22), driving out the Judeans. The chapter is showing us the historical setting of one of the Bible's most famous prophetic moments. This is precisely the crisis of Isaiah 7: as the two kings besiege Jerusalem and Ahaz's heart 'was moved... as the trees of the wood are moved with the wind,' the prophet Isaiah came to Ahaz with the LORD's promise — 'Fear not... it shall not stand' — and offered him a sign, which the faithless Ahaz refused, prompting Isaiah's great Immanuel prophecy ('a virgin shall conceive'). The chapter (read alongside Isaiah 7) is teaching us about the decisive test of faith that comes in crisis: the LORD offered Ahaz deliverance and a sign, asking only that he trust; Ahaz, as the next verses show, chose instead to trust Assyria. The chapter is teaching us that the besieged king's real problem was not the two kings outside the wall but the unbelief inside his own heart — the LORD had promised the coalition would fail, and indeed 'they could not overcome him,' yet Ahaz would not rest in that promise.

So Ahaz sent messengers to Tiglathpileser king of Assyria, saying, I am thy servant and thy son: come up, and save me out of the hand of the king of Syria, and out of the hand of the king of Israel, which rise up against me. And Ahaz took the silver and gold that was found in the house of the LORD, and in the treasures of the king's house, and sent it for a present to the king of Assyria. And the king of Assyria hearkened unto him: for the king of Assyria went up against Damascus, and took it, and carried the people of it captive to Kir, and slew Rezin. Guidance: Ahaz trusts Assyria instead of the LORD. Rejecting Isaiah's call to faith, Ahaz turns to the superpower. He sends to Tiglath-pileser with a message of abject submission: 'I am thy servant and thy son: come up, and save me.' The words are theologically appalling — 'thy servant and thy son' is covenant-language; Ahaz offers Assyria the loyalty and dependence that belong to the LORD alone. He funds the appeal by stripping 'the silver and gold that was found in the house of the LORD, and in the treasures of the king's house' and sending it as tribute. Assyria responds — not out of kindness but because crushing Aram served its own imperial ambition: Tiglath-pileser takes Damascus, deports its people to Kir, and kills Rezin. The immediate threat to Judah is removed. The chapter is showing us the tragedy of unbelief 'working.' On the surface, Ahaz's policy succeeds — the coalition is destroyed, Rezin is dead, Jerusalem is saved. But it succeeded by making Judah a vassal of Assyria, draining the temple to bribe a pagan empire, and refusing the LORD who had freely promised deliverance. (2 Chr 28:20-21 notes that Tiglath-pileser ultimately 'distressed him, but strengthened him not' — the alliance brought bondage, not real help.) The chapter is teaching us the deepest lesson of the Ahaz crisis: faithless solutions can produce short-term results while incurring catastrophic long-term costs. Ahaz got rid of two small kings by submitting to a monster that would dominate the region for a century and eventually threaten Judah itself. The chapter is teaching us that when we refuse to trust God and instead 'buy' security from worldly powers, we trade freedom for bondage and the LORD's free deliverance for a debt we cannot repay. Isaiah had said 'if ye will not believe, surely ye shall not be established' — and Ahaz's choice to trust Assyria rather than God set Judah on the long road that leads, eventually, to Babylon.

And king Ahaz went to Damascus to meet Tiglathpileser king of Assyria, and saw an altar that was at Damascus: and king Ahaz sent to Urijah the priest the fashion of the altar, and the pattern of it, according to all the workmanship thereof. And Urijah the priest built an altar according to all that king Ahaz had sent from Damascus: so Urijah the priest made it against king Ahaz came from Damascus. And when the king was come from Damascus, the king saw the altar: and the king approached to the altar, and offered thereon. And he burnt his burnt offering and his meat offering, and poured his drink offering, and sprinkled the blood of his peace offerings, upon the altar. Guidance: The pagan altar imported into the temple. Ahaz travels to Damascus to pay homage to his new overlord Tiglath-pileser. There he sees an altar — likely an Assyrian-style altar set up in conquered Damascus — and is so taken with it that he sends its exact design and workmanship to Urijah the high priest in Jerusalem with orders to build a replica. Urijah complies, and by the time Ahaz returns the new altar stands ready. The king himself approaches it and offers the full range of sacrifices on it — burnt offering, grain offering, drink offering, and the blood of peace offerings. The chapter is showing us idolatry entering the very heart of Judah's worship. Ahaz does not merely tolerate high places (as the flawed-but-good kings did); he redesigns the temple's central altar after a pagan model he admired in the capital of his pagan master. The altar of the LORD, built to divine specification, is displaced by a copy of an Assyrian shrine. The chapter is teaching us how cultural and political subservience corrupts worship: having made himself Assyria's 'servant and son,' Ahaz now imports Assyria's religion, remaking the LORD's house in the image of his idol-overlord. The chapter is also exposing the tragic complicity of the priest. Urijah — the high priest, guardian of the LORD's worship — builds the pagan altar 'according to all that king Ahaz had sent' without recorded protest. The chapter is teaching us that when religious leaders comply with a powerful man's corruptions rather than resist them, the corruption becomes institutionalized. A faithful priest would have refused; Urijah obeyed the king over the LORD, and the temple was defiled with his cooperation.

And he brought also the brasen altar, which was before the LORD, from the forefront of the house, from between the altar and the house of the LORD, and put it on the north side of the altar. And king Ahaz commanded Urijah the priest, saying, Upon the great altar burn the morning burnt offering, and the evening meat offering, and the king's burnt sacrifice, and his meat offering, with the burnt offering of all the people of the land, and their meat offering, and their drink offerings; and sprinkle upon it all the blood of the burnt offering, and all the blood of the sacrifice: and the brasen altar shall be for me to enquire by. Thus did Urijah the priest, according to all that king Ahaz commanded. Guidance: The LORD's altar shoved aside. Ahaz completes the displacement. The original bronze altar (made for Solomon's temple, the LORD's appointed altar) had stood in front of the temple, between the altar-court and the sanctuary. Ahaz moves it aside to the north and installs his new Damascus-style altar in the place of honor. He then issues detailed commands: the 'great altar' (his new pagan-pattern one) is to be used for ALL the regular and public sacrifices — morning and evening offerings, the king's offerings, all the people's offerings, all the blood. The LORD's bronze altar is demoted to Ahaz's private use, 'for me to enquire by' (perhaps for divination, or kept as a personal relic). And again: 'Thus did Urijah the priest, according to all that king Ahaz commanded.' The chapter is showing us the complete inversion of true worship. The altar the LORD appointed is pushed to the margins; the altar Ahaz copied from a pagan shrine takes center stage and receives all the nation's worship. The king has, in effect, replaced the LORD's prescribed worship with one of his own devising, modeled on his idol-master's religion. The chapter is teaching us the subtle progression of corrupted worship: it does not always announce itself as outright Baal-worship; sometimes it keeps the LORD's name and the temple's forms while quietly substituting a man-made design for the God-given one. Ahaz still calls it worship of the LORD, still uses the temple — but on his own altar, by his own pattern, under his own authority. The chapter is teaching us that worship done our way, however sincere or impressive, that displaces what God has appointed, is not acceptable worship but idolatry dressed in temple clothes. The repeated note that Urijah simply obeyed underscores the collapse of priestly faithfulness — the one man positioned to say 'this may not be' said nothing.

Thus did Urijah the priest, according to all that king Ahaz commanded. Guidance: The LORD's altar shoved aside. Ahaz completes the displacement. The original bronze altar (made for Solomon's temple, the LORD's appointed altar) had stood in front of the temple, between the altar-court and the sanctuary. Ahaz moves it aside to the north and installs his new Damascus-style altar in the place of honor. He then issues detailed commands: the 'great altar' (his new pagan-pattern one) is to be used for ALL the regular and public sacrifices — morning and evening offerings, the king's offerings, all the people's offerings, all the blood. The LORD's bronze altar is demoted to Ahaz's private use, 'for me to enquire by' (perhaps for divination, or kept as a personal relic). And again: 'Thus did Urijah the priest, according to all that king Ahaz commanded.' The chapter is showing us the complete inversion of true worship. The altar the LORD appointed is pushed to the margins; the altar Ahaz copied from a pagan shrine takes center stage and receives all the nation's worship. The king has, in effect, replaced the LORD's prescribed worship with one of his own devising, modeled on his idol-master's religion. The chapter is teaching us the subtle progression of corrupted worship: it does not always announce itself as outright Baal-worship; sometimes it keeps the LORD's name and the temple's forms while quietly substituting a man-made design for the God-given one. Ahaz still calls it worship of the LORD, still uses the temple — but on his own altar, by his own pattern, under his own authority. The chapter is teaching us that worship done our way, however sincere or impressive, that displaces what God has appointed, is not acceptable worship but idolatry dressed in temple clothes. The repeated note that Urijah simply obeyed underscores the collapse of priestly faithfulness — the one man positioned to say 'this may not be' said nothing.

And he brought also the brasen altar, which was before the LORD, from the forefront of the house, from between the altar and the house of the LORD, and put it on the north side of the altar. And king Ahaz commanded Urijah the priest, saying, Upon the great altar burn the morning burnt offering, and the evening meat offering, and the king's burnt sacrifice, and his meat offering, with the burnt offering of all the people of the land, and their meat offering, and their drink offerings; and sprinkle upon it all the blood of the burnt offering, and all the blood of the sacrifice: and the brasen altar shall be for me to enquire by. Thus did Urijah the priest, according to all that king Ahaz commanded. Guidance: The LORD's altar shoved aside. Ahaz completes the displacement. The original bronze altar (made for Solomon's temple, the LORD's appointed altar) had stood in front of the temple, between the altar-court and the sanctuary. Ahaz moves it aside to the north and installs his new Damascus-style altar in the place of honor. He then issues detailed commands: the 'great altar' (his new pagan-pattern one) is to be used for ALL the regular and public sacrifices — morning and evening offerings, the king's offerings, all the people's offerings, all the blood. The LORD's bronze altar is demoted to Ahaz's private use, 'for me to enquire by' (perhaps for divination, or kept as a personal relic). And again: 'Thus did Urijah the priest, according to all that king Ahaz commanded.' The chapter is showing us the complete inversion of true worship. The altar the LORD appointed is pushed to the margins; the altar Ahaz copied from a pagan shrine takes center stage and receives all the nation's worship. The king has, in effect, replaced the LORD's prescribed worship with one of his own devising, modeled on his idol-master's religion. The chapter is teaching us the subtle progression of corrupted worship: it does not always announce itself as outright Baal-worship; sometimes it keeps the LORD's name and the temple's forms while quietly substituting a man-made design for the God-given one. Ahaz still calls it worship of the LORD, still uses the temple — but on his own altar, by his own pattern, under his own authority. The chapter is teaching us that worship done our way, however sincere or impressive, that displaces what God has appointed, is not acceptable worship but idolatry dressed in temple clothes. The repeated note that Urijah simply obeyed underscores the collapse of priestly faithfulness — the one man positioned to say 'this may not be' said nothing.

And king Ahaz cut off the borders of the bases, and removed the laver from off them; and took down the sea from off the brasen oxen that were under it, and put it upon a pavement of stones. And the covert for the sabbath that they had built in the house, and the king's entry without, turned he from the house of the LORD for the king of Assyria. Guidance: Dismantling the temple to pay the empire. Ahaz now strips the temple's costly furnishings — almost certainly to extract their bronze for Assyrian tribute. He cuts the decorative panels off the wheeled bases (the elaborate bronze stands Solomon had made, 1 Kgs 7:27-37), removes the lavers, and takes the great bronze 'sea' off the twelve bronze oxen that supported it, setting it on a plain stone pavement instead. He also alters the temple's sabbath-canopy and the royal entryway 'for the king of Assyria' — reconfiguring the LORD's house to accommodate his Assyrian overlord. The chapter is showing us the LORD's temple being cannibalized and rearranged to serve a pagan empire. Having imported a pagan altar, Ahaz now plunders the temple's God-given craftsmanship — the works made by divine wisdom through Solomon's artisans — melting beauty into tribute and reshaping the sanctuary around the demands of his master in Nineveh. The chapter is teaching us the full trajectory of trusting in worldly power instead of God: it does not merely supplement true worship, it consumes it. Ahaz began by refusing to trust the LORD (the Syro-Ephraimite crisis), proceeded to buy Assyrian protection with temple gold, imported a pagan altar, demoted the LORD's altar, and now dismantles the temple itself to keep paying his protector. Each step required the next. The chapter is teaching us that there is no neutral ground in trust: the king who would not lean on the LORD ends up tearing down the LORD's house to prop up his alliance with the world. What begins as a political calculation ends as the systematic desecration of everything holy.

He also alters the temple's sabbath-canopy and the royal entryway 'for the king of Assyria' — reconfiguring the LORD's house to accommodate his Assyrian overlord. The chapter is showing us the LORD's temple being cannibalized and rearranged to serve a pagan empire. Having imported a pagan altar, Ahaz now plunders the temple's God-given craftsmanship — the works made by divine wisdom through Solomon's artisans — melting beauty into tribute and reshaping the sanctuary around the demands of his master in Nineveh. The chapter is teaching us the full trajectory of trusting in worldly power instead of God: it does not merely supplement true worship, it consumes it. Ahaz began by refusing to trust the LORD (the Syro-Ephraimite crisis), proceeded to buy Assyrian protection with temple gold, imported a pagan altar, demoted the LORD's altar, and now dismantles the temple itself to keep paying his protector. Each step required the next. The chapter is teaching us that there is no neutral ground in trust: the king who would not lean on the LORD ends up tearing down the LORD's house to prop up his alliance with the world. What begins as a political calculation ends as the systematic desecration of everything holy.

Now the rest of the acts of Ahaz which he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? And Ahaz slept with his fathers, and was buried with his fathers in the city of David: and Hezekiah his son reigned in his stead. Guidance: Ahaz's close — and the dawn of hope. The faithless reign ends with the standard formula. (2 Chr 28:27 adds the telling detail that though buried in Jerusalem, he was 'not brought into the sepulchres of the kings of Israel' — a posthumous dishonor for his apostasy.) But the closing words carry the chapter's one ray of light: 'Hezekiah his son reigned in his stead.' Out of the worst Judean reign yet comes one of the best kings in the nation's history. The chapter is closing the dark Ahaz era while quietly announcing the great reformer to come. Hezekiah — who will trust the LORD where his father trusted Assyria, who will cleanse and reopen the temple his father defiled, who will face the Assyrian threat with prayer instead of tribute (chs 18-20) — is the son and successor of this idolater. The chapter is teaching us a hope that runs against the grain of generational decline: just as a good father (Jotham) produced a wicked son (Ahaz), so a wicked father (Ahaz) can produce a godly son (Hezekiah). The covenant lamp, nearly smothered under Ahaz, is about to burn bright again. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's purposes are not held hostage to one bad generation — He can raise up a reformer from the household of an apostate, and the very temple Ahaz desecrated will be restored by the son Ahaz raised. The darkest reign is followed immediately by one of the brightest; the chapter ends Ahaz's story not in despair but in the announcement of the deliverer-king who will undo his father's ruin.
